:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 35 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 1% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

Individuals are eligible for parole release when they have served a certain percentage of their sentence based on the felony class, felony count, and whether or not their crime was a crime of violence. If serving a consecutive sentence, the time to parole will be added together to determine a parole eligibility date. As will be discussed further below, individuals who committed crimes after July 1, 1996 must be released when eligible unless they have failed to substantially comply with their individual program directive while incarcerated. Experts within the state estimate that around 80% of individuals are released at their presumptive date. The parole date is calculated on admission. Inmates may request review of their established parole date. The Board must provide the inmate an opportunity to present their factual and legal arguments in order to determine the true parole date.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 27 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 77% of the parole-eligible prison population in prison for new crimes.

``` ::: :::

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Descriptive Statistics

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Race, ethincity, age, and gender

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Relative Rate Index

The relative rate index (RRI) provides a measure of disparity in outcomes for different racial and ethnic groups by comparing the rates at which individuals of different races and ethnic groups experience incarceration. The rate for each specific racial and ethnic group is computed by dividing the population of interest within that group by the total state population for that particular group.

To calculate the RRI, the rate of each outcome of interest for individuals who are Black non-Hispanic and Hispanic any race, is divided by the rate at which the outcome occurs for individuals who are White. A RRI of 1 indicates no disparity. RRIs greater than 1 indicate the outcome occurs more frequently for individuals who are Black or Hispanic, and an RRI less than 1 indicates an outcome occurs less frequently than for individuals who are White.
